did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluates AIaMD, an artificial intelligence as a medical device (AIaMD) used for the screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for skin cancer. The study reports clinical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, negative predictive value) obtained by applying this AI model to 3979 lesions in a multicentre prospective clinical performance review.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is a conference abstract (indicated by 'Abstracts i193' and 'Abstract citation ID') presenting original results from a 'prospective, postdeployment, multicentre clinical performance review' of an AI device (AIaMD). It reports new data on clinical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, NPV) derived from assessing 3979 lesions across multiple NHS sites, which constitutes original experimental/observational research rather than a literature review.

title: Multicentre prospective clinical performance analysis of an artificial intelligence as a medical device deployed within urgent suspected skin cancer pathways
brief_summary: This multicentre prospective clinical performance review evaluated AIaMD, an artificial intelligence as a medical device (AIaMD) for screening and triaging skin lesions suspicious for cancer, deployed across four NHS sites. The study assessed 3979 lesions in patients referred via urgent suspected skin cancer pathways between April 2022 and November 2023, with outcomes confirmed by histology or teledermatology. AIaMD demonstrated a negative predictive value of 99.8% and sensitivities of 97% for melanoma, 96.6% for all skin cancers, and 86.3% for premalignancy. The specificity for benign lesions was 74.9% overall, though it dropped to 27.6% when considering only biopsy-confirmed benign cases. Implementation of the AI device was associated with a 10% reduction in biopsies and an 11-day reduction in wait times to first appointment. The results suggest that integrating AIaMD can improve diagnostic accuracy and pathway efficiency in urgent skin cancer referrals.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of skin lesions (e.g., melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, premalignant, benign).
all_task_types.details: The AI device (AIaMD) performs screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for cancer. The study reports classification performance metrics including sensitivity (for melanoma, all skin cancers, and premalignancy) and specificity (for benign lesions), indicating discrete diagnostic category prediction.

datasets_used.name: AIaMD Multicentre Prospective Clinical Performance Review Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective, postdeployment, multicentre clinical performance review of the AIaMD device deployed across four NHS sites (including Royal Cornwall Hospitals Trust, Ninewells Hospital, Chelsea & Westminster Hospital, and Hetherington Group Practice) as part of the NHS AI in Health and Care Award. The cohort consists of patients referred via urgent suspected skin cancer (USC) pathways between April 2022 and November 2023. Outcomes were confirmed by histology reports for cancerous lesions and histology or consultant teledermatology assessment for premalignant and benign lesions.

use.testing: Used for clinical performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, NPV) of the AIaMD device.
use.other: Postdeployment performance monitoring and pathway efficiency analysis (biopsy reduction, wait time reduction).

datasets_used.name: Community Lesion Imaging Clinic (CLIC) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A subset of patients reviewed in a low-cost community imaging lesion clinic (CLIC) service using band 3 healthcare assistants, iPads, and Dermlite DL4 dermoscopes. Data was entered into the Islacare cloud-based platform. This cohort is distinct from the main AIaMD deployment cohort in terms of data collection method and timeframe.

use.other: Implementation and initial results reporting for a tele-triage model using community imaging clinics.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Reduction in wait times to first appointment

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper presents descriptive clinical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, NPV) and pathway efficiency outcomes without statistical significance testing.

AIaMD Multicentre Prospective Clinical P.metric: Clinical Performance Metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV) and Pathway Efficiency
AIaMD Multicentre Prospective Clinical P.notes: The study evaluated 3979 lesions across four NHS sites. Outcomes were confirmed by histology for cancerous lesions and histology or teledermatology for premalignant and benign lesions. One site reported the reductions in wait times, biopsies, and follow-up appointments.

Community Lesion Imaging Clinic (CLIC) C.notes: This cohort involved a low-cost tele-triage model using band 3 healthcare assistants, iPads, and Dermlite DL4 dermoscopes. The paper does not report diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity/specificity) for this specific CLIC cohort, only implementation details and demographics.

architecture_used.main_model: AIaMD (Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device)

architecture_details.model_family: Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD)
architecture_details.deployment_context: Deployed at four NHS sites as part of the NHS AI in Health and Care Award

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Diagnostic category assessment (screening, triage, and assessment of skin lesions suspicious for skin cancer)

conclusions: The AIaMD device demonstrated high clinical performance in a multicentre prospective review, with a negative predictive value of 99.8% for ruling out cancer.
AIaMD showed high sensitivity for detecting melanoma (97%), all skin cancers (96.6%), and premalignant lesions (86.3%).
The specificity of AIaMD for benign lesions was 74.9% overall, but dropped to 27.6% when considering only biopsy-confirmed benign cases.

Implementation of AIaMD was associated with pathway efficiency improvements, including an 11-day reduction in average wait time to first appointment, a 10% reduction in biopsies, and a 13% reduction in routine follow-up appointments.
The integration of AIaMD into urgent suspected skin cancer diagnostic pathways could significantly improve diagnostic accuracy by removing unnecessary specialist review of benign lesions.
The study highlights the urgent need for improved diagnostic skills in primary care for recognizing common skin lesions, as 84% of urgent suspected cancer referrals were either benign or nonurgent.
important_remarks: Potential conflict of interest: Two authors are employed by one of the deployment sites and three authors are employed by the AI provider (Skin Analytics).
Funding source disclosure: Part of the deployment period was funded as part of the NHS AI in Health and Care Award.
Low specificity for biopsy-confirmed benign lesions: Specificity dropped to 27.6% (105 of 381) when considering only biopsy-confirmed benign cases, compared to 74.9% overall.
High proportion of non-cancer referrals: 84% of cases referred as urgent suspected cancer were either benign or nonurgent, highlighting a need for improved primary care diagnostic skills.
Study design limitations: The paper is a conference abstract presenting postmarket service evaluation data; it lacks detailed statistical methods or hypothesis testing.
Deployment context: The study evaluates a UKCA class IIa-approved AI device deployed across four NHS sites, which may limit generalizability to other healthcare systems.
